cards.front: What happens if an if statement's condition evaluates to false and there is no else clause?
cards.back: The if statement is skipped entirely and execution continues with the next statement after the if block.

cards.text: A {{c1::block}} (also called a {{c2::compound statement}}) is a group of statements treated as a single statement, defined using curly braces {{c3::{ }}}. }}}

cards.concept: Why always use blocks with if statements
cards.explanation:
Even when an if statement only needs to execute a single statement, you should always use blocks (curly braces) because:
  • It prevents bugs when adding more statements later
  • It makes the code more readable and less ambiguous
  • It's consistent and reduces cognitive load
  • It prevents the "dangling else" problem

cards.front: What is the scope of variables declared inside a block?
cards.back: Variables declared inside a block have block scope - they are only accessible from the point of declaration until the end of the block.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Variable y is out of scope. It was declared inside the if block and can't be accessed outside.
cards.explanation: Variables declared in a block have block scope and are destroyed when the block ends. To use y after the if statement, declare it before the if block.

cards.text:
Nested if statements: {{c1::if (condition1)}}
{
    {{c2::if (condition2)}}
    {
        // both conditions true
    }
}

cards.front: Should you use nested if statements or logical AND (&&) when checking multiple conditions that must all be true?
cards.back:
Use logical AND (&&) in a single if statement. It's more readable and efficient:
if (condition1 && condition2) is better than nested ifs.

cards.text: The {{c1::else}} clause is attached to the closest unmatched {{c2::if}} statement in the same block.

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: There's a semicolon after the if condition, creating a null statement. The block always executes regardless of the condition.
cards.explanation: The semicolon ends the if statement with an empty statement. The block is not part of the if - it always executes. This is a common and subtle bug.

cards.text: A {{c1::null statement}} is a statement consisting of just a semicolon. It does {{c2::nothing}}.

cards.front: Where should you place the opening brace of a block?
cards.back: On the same line as the statement that opens the block (e.g., if, while, for). This is the most common C++ style convention.
